title: Fenwick Tree (Binary Indexed Tree)
code: public void Update(int i, long delta)
public long RangeSum(int l, int r) => PrefixSum(r) - PrefixSum(l - 1);

frames.caption: Original array a[1..8] = [1..8]. The Fenwick tree starts empty; index 0 is an unused ghost.
frames.note: Prefix sums answer a range in O(1) but cost O(n) to update. A Fenwick tree makes BOTH a prefix query and a point update O(log n), so it wins the moment the array changes between queries. It is 1-indexed because index 0 is the loop's stop sentinel.

frames.caption: Build tree[1] = sum a[1..1] = 1   (block length lowbit(1)=1).
frames.note: The Fenwick tree is one array where tree[i] holds the sum of a contiguous block of the original array ENDING at i, whose length is lowbit(i) = i & -i (i's lowest set bit). Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. These nested blocks are why both query and update take O(log n) — they tile the array like the binary digits of an index.

frames.caption: Build tree[2] = sum a[1..2] = 3   (block length lowbit(2)=2).
frames.note: The Fenwick tree is one array where tree[i] holds the sum of a contiguous block of the original array ENDING at i, whose length is lowbit(i) = i & -i (i's lowest set bit). Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. These nested blocks are why both query and update take O(log n) — they tile the array like the binary digits of an index.

frames.caption: Build tree[3] = sum a[3..3] = 3   (block length lowbit(3)=1).
frames.note: The Fenwick tree is one array where tree[i] holds the sum of a contiguous block of the original array ENDING at i, whose length is lowbit(i) = i & -i (i's lowest set bit). Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. frames.caption: Build tree[6] = sum a[5..6] = 11   (block length lowbit(6)=2).

Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. frames.caption: Build tree[7] = sum a[7..7] = 7   (block length lowbit(7)=1).

Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. frames.caption: Build tree[8] = sum a[1..8] = 36   (block length lowbit(8)=8).
frames.note: The Fenwick tree is one array where tree[i] holds the sum of a contiguous block of the original array ENDING at i, whose length is lowbit(i) = i & -i (i's lowest set bit). Powers of two own long blocks (tree[8] covers all 8, tree[4] covers 4), odd indices own a single element. These nested blocks are why both query and update take O(log n) — they tile the array like the binary digits of an index.

frames.caption: Built: tree = [_, 1, 3, 3, 10, 5, 11, 7, 36].
frames.note: Each tree[i] now owns its block; together the powers-of-two blocks let any prefix be assembled from at most log n of them. Next: a prefix query and a point update, the two O(log n) walks.

frames.caption: prefix(7): i=7, add tree[7]=7 → sum=7. Then i -= lowbit(7)=1.
frames.note: A prefix query sum(a[1..i]) walks DOWN: add tree[i], then strip the lowest set bit (i -= i & -i) to jump to the block just below, and repeat until i hits 0. Each step clears one set bit, so there are at most log n jumps. The blocks visited are disjoint and exactly tile [1..i].

frames.caption: prefix(7): i=6, add tree[6]=11 → sum=18. Then i -= lowbit(6)=2.
frames.note: A prefix query sum(a[1..i]) walks DOWN: add tree[i], then strip the lowest set bit (i -= i & -i) to jump to the block just below, and repeat until i hits 0. Each step clears one set bit, so there are at most log n jumps. The blocks visited are disjoint and exactly tile [1..i].

frames.caption: prefix(7): i=4, add tree[4]=10 → sum=28. Then i -= lowbit(4)=4.
frames.note: A prefix query sum(a[1..i]) walks DOWN: add tree[i], then strip the lowest set bit (i -= i & -i) to jump to the block just below, and repeat until i hits 0. Each step clears one set bit, so there are at most log n jumps. The blocks visited are disjoint and exactly tile [1..i].

frames.caption: i = 0 → stop. prefix(7) = 28  (sum of a[1..7]).
frames.note: i reached 0, so the walk is done: the visited blocks tile a[1..7] with no overlap, summing to 28. A range sum a[l..r] is just prefix(r) - prefix(l-1) — two O(log n) walks subtracted, the dynamic-array answer to what a static prefix-sum array does in O(1).

frames.caption: Now update a[3] += 2 (3 → 5). Walk UP the tree, adding lowbit each step.
frames.note: A point update changes only the blocks that contain index 3. We find them by repeatedly ADDING the lowest set bit — the inverse of the query walk.

frames.caption: update: tree[3] += 2 → 5. Then i += lowbit(3)=1.
frames.note: A point update a[i] += delta walks UP: add delta to tree[i], then ADD the lowest set bit (i += i & -i) to jump to the next block that CONTAINS i, and repeat past n. These are exactly the blocks whose range covers index i — the mirror image of the query's subtraction. At most log n blocks change, so updates are O(log n), unlike a prefix-sum array's O(n) rebuild.

frames.caption: update: tree[4] += 2 → 12. Then i += lowbit(4)=4.
frames.note: A point update a[i] += delta walks UP: add delta to tree[i], then ADD the lowest set bit (i += i & -i) to jump to the next block that CONTAINS i, and repeat past n. These are exactly the blocks whose range covers index i — the mirror image of the query's subtraction. At most log n blocks change, so updates are O(log n), unlike a prefix-sum array's O(n) rebuild.

frames.caption: update: tree[8] += 2 → 38. Then i += lowbit(8)=8.
frames.note: A point update a[i] += delta walks UP: add delta to tree[i], then ADD the lowest set bit (i += i & -i) to jump to the next block that CONTAINS i, and repeat past n. These are exactly the blocks whose range covers index i — the mirror image of the query's subtraction. At most log n blocks change, so updates are O(log n), unlike a prefix-sum array's O(n) rebuild.

frames.caption: Check: prefix(6) now = tree[6]+tree[4] = 11+12 = 23  (a[1..6] after the +2).
frames.note: Both operations run in O(log n) time after an O(n) build, in a single array of size n+1. That is the Fenwick tree's whole pitch: cheap point updates AND cheap prefix/range sums on a changing array. When the aggregate is a sum (invertible), the BIT is smaller and faster than a segment tree; for min/max or range updates, reach for a segment tree instead.
